aiTitle: 調查 SessionEnd hook 錯誤

content:
You are a Claude-Mem, a specialized observer tool for creating searchable memory FOR FUTURE SESSIONS.

CRITICAL: Record what was LEARNED/BUILT/FIXED/DEPLOYED/CONFIGURED, not what you (the observer) are doing.

You do not have access to tools. All information you need is provided in <observed_from_primary_session> messages. Create observations from what you observe - no investigation needed.

<observed_from_primary_session>
  <user_request>為何我剛才結束sesions時會出現錯誤
SessionEnd hook [node "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/session-lifecycle-hook.mjs" SessionEnd] failed: Hook cancelled</user_request>
  <requested_at>2026-07-01</requested_at>
</observed_from_primary_session>

Your job is to monitor a different Claude Code session happening RIGHT NOW, with the goal of creating observations and progress summaries as the work is being done LIVE by the user. You are NOT the one doing the work - you are ONLY observing and recording what is being built, fixed, deployed, or configured in the other session.

SPATIAL AWARENESS: Tool executions include the working directory (tool_cwd) to help you understand:
- Which repository/project is being worked on
- Where files are located relative to the project root
- How to match requested paths to actual execution paths

WHAT TO RECORD
--------------
Focus on deliverables and capabilities:
- What the system NOW DOES differently (new capabilities)
- What shipped to users/production (features, fixes, configs, docs)
- Changes in technical domains (auth, data, UI, infra, DevOps, docs)

Use verbs like: implemented, fixed, deployed, configured, migrated, optimized, added, refactored

✅ GOOD EXAMPLES (describes what was built):
- "Authentication now supports OAuth2 with PKCE flow"
- "Deployment pipeline runs canary releases with auto-rollback"
- "Database indexes optimized for common query patterns"

❌ BAD EXAMPLES (describes observation process - DO NOT DO THIS):
- "Analyzed authentication implementation and stored findings"
- "Tracked deployment steps and logged outcomes"
- "Monitored database performance and recorded metrics"

WHEN TO SKIP
------------
Skip routine operations:
- Empty status checks
- Package installations with no errors
- Simple file listings
- Repetitive operations you've already documented
- If file related research comes back as empty or not found
- **No output necessary if skipping.**

OUTPUT FORMAT
-------------
Output observations using this XML structure:

```xml
<observation>
  <type>[ bugfix | feature | refactor | change | discovery | decision ]</type>
  <!--
    **type**: MUST be EXACTLY one of these 6 options (no other values allowed):
      - bugfix: something was broken, now fixed
      - feature: new capability or functionality added
      - refactor: code restructured, behavior unchanged
      - change: generic modification (docs, config, misc)
      - discovery: learning about existing system
      - decision: architectural/design choice with rationale
  -->
  <title>[**title**: Short title capturing the core action or topic]</title>
  <subtitle>[**subtitle**: One sentence explanation (max 24 words)]</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
  </facts>
  <!--
    **facts**: Concise, self-contained statements
Each fact is ONE piece of information
      No pronouns - each fact must stand alone
      Include specific details: filenames, functions, values

**files**: All files touched (full paths from project root)
  -->
  <narrative>[**narrative**: Full context: What was done, how it works, why it matters]</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
  </concepts>
  <!--
    **concepts**: 2-5 knowledge-type categories. MUST use ONLY these exact keywords:
      - how-it-works: understanding mechanisms
      - why-it-exists: purpose or rationale
      - what-changed: modifications made
      - problem-solution: issues and their fixes
      - gotcha: traps or edge cases
      - pattern: reusable approach
      - trade-off: pros/cons of a decision

    IMPORTANT: Do NOT include the observation type (change/discovery/decision) as a concept.
    Types and concepts are separate dimensions.
  -->
  <files_read>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```


IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this OBSERVATIONS from tool use messages - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the observation content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful observations.

Remember that we record these observations as a way of helping us stay on track with our progress, and to help us keep important decisions and changes at the forefront of our minds! :) Thank you so much for your help!

MEMORY PROCESSING START
=======================

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.thinking:
The user is asking about an error that occurred when ending a session. The error message is:

"SessionEnd hook [node "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/session-lifecycle-hook.mjs" SessionEnd] failed: Hook cancelled"

This is a memory agent observing another session. I need to create an observation about this issue that was encountered. Let me analyze:

1. **What happened**: A SessionEnd hook failed with a "Hook cancelled" error
2. **Type**: This appears to be a "discovery" - the user discovered/encountered an error
3. **What was learned**: The session lifecycle hook (specifically SessionEnd) failed when trying to end a session

However, I need to be careful here. The user is just asking WHY this error occurred - they haven't necessarily fixed it or made any changes yet. This is more of a problem report or issue discovery.

Let me structure this as a discovery observation about the SessionEnd hook failure.

Key facts:
- SessionEnd hook failed when ending a session
- Error message indicates "Hook cancelled"
- The hook script is located at "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/session-lifecycle-hook.mjs"
- The hook was attempting to execute the SessionEnd lifecycle event

This is a discovery of an issue/problem with the session lifecycle hooks.

content.text:
```xml
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>SessionEnd Hook Failure on Session Termination</title>
  <subtitle>SessionEnd lifecycle hook in session-lifecycle-hook.mjs fails with "Hook cancelled" error when ending sessions</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>SessionEnd hook fails when attempting to end Claude Code sessions</fact>
    <fact>Error message reports "Hook cancelled" for the node process executing session-lifecycle-hook.mjs</fact>
    <fact>Hook script located at ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/session-lifecycle-hook.mjs</fact>
    <fact>Failure occurs during the SessionEnd lifecycle event execution</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>When ending Claude Code sessions, the SessionEnd lifecycle hook fails with a "Hook cancelled" error. The hook is configured to run a Node.js script at ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/session-lifecycle-hook.mjs with the SessionEnd parameter. The cancellation suggests the hook process was interrupted or terminated before completion, potentially due to timeout, process termination, or an unhandled error within the hook script itself. This prevents proper session cleanup or any post-session actions configured in the lifecycle hook.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```

\\\\\\\"${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/session-lifecycle-hook.mjs\\\\\\\" SessionStart\\\",\\n    \\\"SessionEnd\\\": [\\n            \\\"command\\\": \\\"node \\\\\\\"${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/session-lifecycle-hook.mjs\\\\\\\" SessionEnd\\\",\\n\\n===== hook script =====\\n#!/usr/bin/env node\\r\\n\\r\\nimport fs from \\\"node:fs\\\";\\r\\nimport process from \\\"node:process\\\";\\r\\n\\r\\nimport { terminateProcessTree } from \\\"./lib/process.mjs\\\";\\r\\nimport { BROKER_ENDPOINT_ENV } from \\\"./lib/app-server.mjs\\\";\\r\\nimport {\\r\\n  clearBrokerSession,\\r\\n  LOG_FILE_ENV,\\r\\n  loadBrokerSession,\\r\\n  PID_FILE_ENV,\\r\\n  sendBrokerShutdown,\\r\\n  teardownBrokerSession\\r\\n} from \\\"./lib/broker-lifecycle.mjs\\\";\\r\\nimport { loadState, resolveStateFile, saveState } from \\\"./lib/state.mjs\\\";\\r\\nimport { resolveWorkspaceRoot } from \\\"./lib/workspace.mjs\\\";\\r\\n\\r\\nexport const SESSION_ID_ENV = \\\"CODEX_COMPANION_SESSION_ID\\\";\\r\\nconst PLUGIN_DATA_ENV = \\\"CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA\\\";\\r\\n\\r\\nfunction readHookInput() {\\r\\n  const raw = fs.readFileSync(0, \\\"utf8\\\").trim();\\r\\n  if (!raw) {\\r\\n    return {};\\r\\n  }\\r\\n  return JSON.parse(raw);\\r\\n}\\r\\n\\r\\nfunction shellEscape(value) {\\r\\n  return `'${String(value).replace(/'/g, `'\\\\\\\"'\\\\\\\"'`)}'`;\\r\\n}\\r\\n\\r\\nfunction appendEnvVar(name, value) {\\r\\n  if (!process.env.CLAUDE_ENV_FILE || value == null || value === \\\"\\\") {\\r\\n    return;\\r\\n  }\\r\\n  fs.appendFileSync(process.env.CLAUDE_ENV_FILE, `export ${name}=${shellEscape(value)}\\\\n`, \\\"utf8\\\");\\r\\n}\\r\\n\\r\\nfunction cleanupSessionJobs(cwd, sessionId) {\\r\\n  if (!cwd || !sessionId) {\\r\\n    return;\\r\\n  }\\r\\n\\r\\n  const workspaceRoot = resolveWorkspaceRoot(cwd);\\r\\n  const stateFile = resolveStateFile(workspaceRoot);\\r\\n  if (!fs.existsSync(stateFile)) {\\r\\n    return;\\r\\n  }\\r\\n\\r\\n  const state = loadState(workspaceRoot);\\r\\n  const removedJobs = state.jobs.filter((job) => job.sessionId === sessionId);\\r\\n  if (removedJobs.length === 0) {\\r\\n    return;\\r\\n  }\\r\\n\\r\\n  for (const job of removedJobs) {\\r\\n    const stillRunning = job.status === \\\"queued\\\" || job.status === \\\"running\\\";\\r\\n    if (!stillRunning) {\\r\\n      continue;\\r\\n    }\\r\\n    try {\\r\\n      terminateProcessTree(job.pid ?? Number.NaN);\\r\\n    } catch {\\r\\n      // Ignore teardown failures during session shutdown.\\r\\n    }\\r\\n  }\\r\\n\\r\\n  saveState(workspaceRoot, {\\r\\n    ...state,\\r\\n    jobs: state.jobs.filter((job) => job.sessionId !== sessionId)\\r\\n  });\\r\\n}\\r\\n\\r\\nfunction handleSessionStart(input) {\\r\\n  appendEnvVar(SESSION_ID_ENV, input.session_id);\\r\\n  appendEnvVar(PLUGIN_DATA_ENV, process.env[PLUGIN_DATA_ENV]);\\r\\n}\\r\\n\\r\\nasync function handleSessionEnd(input) {\\r\\n  const cwd = input.cwd || process.cwd();\\r\\n  const brokerSession =\\r\\n    loadBrokerSession(cwd) ??\\r\\n    (process.env[BROKER_ENDPOINT_ENV]\\r\\n      ? {\\r\\n          endpoint: process.env[BROKER_ENDPOINT_ENV],\\r\\n          pidFile: process.env[PID_FILE_ENV] ?? null,\\r\\n          logFile: process.env[LOG_FILE_ENV] ?? null\\r\\n        }\\r\\n      : null);\\r\\n  const brokerEndpoint = brokerSession?.endpoint ?? null;\\r\\n  const pidFile = brokerSession?.pidFile ?? null;\\r\\n  const logFile = brokerSession?.logFile ?? null;\\r\\n  const sessionDir = brokerSession?.sessionDir ?? null;\\r\\n  const pid = brokerSession?.pid ?? null;\\r\\n\\r\\n  if (brokerEndpoint) {\\r\\n    await sendBrokerShutdown(brokerEndpoint);\\r\\n  }\\r\\n\\r\\n  cleanupSessionJobs(cwd, input.session_id || process.env[SESSION_ID_ENV]);\\r\\n  teardownBrokerSession({\\r\\n    endpoint: brokerEndpoint,\\r\\n    pidFile,\\r\\n    logFile,\\r\\n    sessionDir,\\r\\n    pid,\\r\\n    killProcess: terminateProcessTree\\r\\n  });\\r\\n  clearBrokerSession(cwd);\\r\\n}\\r\\n\\r\\nasync function main() {\\r\\n  const input = readHookInput();\\r\\n  const eventName = process.argv[2] ?? input.hook_event_name ?? \\\"\\\";\\r\\n\\r\\n  if (eventName === \\\"SessionStart\\\") {\\r\\n    handleSessionStart(input);\\r\\n    return;\\r\\n  }\\r\\n\\r\\n  if (eventName === \\\"SessionEnd\\\") {\\r\\n    await handleSessionEnd(input);\\r\\n  }\\r\\n}\\r\\n\\r\\nmain().catch((error) => {\\r\\n  process.stderr.write(`${error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error)}\\\\n`);\\r\\n  process.exit(1);\\r\\n});\",\"stderr\":\"\",\"interrupted\":false,\"isImage\":false,\"noOutputExpected\":false}"</outcome>

aiTitle: 調查 SessionEnd hook 錯誤

content.thinking:
The user is investigating why their SessionEnd hook failed. A Bash command was executed to locate settings files and the session-lifecycle-hook.mjs script. 

Key findings:
1. Multiple settings files found:
   - /c/Users/jiunchiwang/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/chrome-devtools-mcp/1.4.0/.gemini/settings.json
   - /c/Users/jiunchiwang/.claude/plugins/marketplaces/thedotmack/.claude/settings.json
   - /c/Users/jiunchiwang/.claude/settings.json
   - /c/Users/jiunchiwang/.claude/settings.local.json

2. Session lifecycle hook script found at TWO locations:
   - /c/Users/jiunchiwang/.claude/plugins/cache/openai-codex/codex/1.0.4/scripts/session-lifecycle-hook.mjs
   - /c/Users/jiunchiwang/.claude/plugins/marketplaces/openai-codex/plugins/codex/scripts/session-lifecycle-hook.mjs

This is a discovery - they're finding where the hook script is located and where settings files are. The hook is part of an "openai-codex" plugin called "codex". This helps understand the environment setup and where the failing hook is coming from.

This is useful information to record because it shows:
- The hook is from a plugin, not native Claude Code
- There are multiple copies of the plugin (cache and marketplaces)
- The plugin is "openai-codex/codex"
- The user has multiple settings files that could contain hook configurations

Multiple settings files were identified that could contain hook configurations, including user-level settings at ~/.claude/settings.json and settings.local.json, plus plugin-specific settings. Understanding that the hook is plugin-sourced (rather than built-in) narrows troubleshooting scope to plugin configuration, permissions, or compatibility issues with the plugin's lifecycle handlers.</narrative>

\\\\\\\"${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/session-lifecycle-hook.mjs\\\\\\\" SessionEnd\\\",\\n\\n===== hook script =====\\n#!/usr/bin/env node\\r\\n\\r\\nimport fs from \\\"node:fs\\\";\\r\\nimport process from \\\"node:process\\\";\\r\\n\\r\\nimport { terminateProcessTree } from \\\"./lib/process.mjs\\\";\\r\\nimport { BROKER_ENDPOINT_ENV } from \\\"./lib/app-server.mjs\\\";\\r\\nimport {\\r\\n  clearBrokerSession,\\r\\n  LOG_FILE_ENV,\\r\\n  loadBrokerSession,\\r\\n  PID_FILE_ENV,\\r\\n  sendBrokerShutdown,\\r\\n  teardownBrokerSession\\r\\n} from \\\"./lib/broker-lifecycle.mjs\\\";\\r\\nimport { loadState, resolveStateFile, saveState } from \\\"./lib/state.mjs\\\";\\r\\nimport { resolveWorkspaceRoot } from \\\"./lib/workspace.mjs\\\";\\r\\n\\r\\nexport const SESSION_ID_ENV = \\\"CODEX_COMPANION_SESSION_ID\\\";\\r\\nconst PLUGIN_DATA_ENV = \\\"CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA\\\";\\r\\n\\r\\nfunction readHookInput() {\\r\\n  const raw = fs.readFileSync(0, \\\"utf8\\\").trim();\\r\\n  if (!raw) {\\r\\n    return {};\\r\\n  }\\r\\n  return JSON.parse(raw);\\r\\n}\\r\\n\\r\\nfunction shellEscape(value) {\\r\\n  return `'${String(value).replace(/'/g, `'\\\\\\\"'\\\\\\\"'`)}'`;\\r\\n}\\r\\n\\r\\nfunction appendEnvVar(name, value) {\\r\\n  if (!process.env.CLAUDE_ENV_FILE || value == null || value === \\\"\\\") {\\r\\n    return;\\r\\n  }\\r\\n  fs.appendFileSync(process.env.CLAUDE_ENV_FILE, `export ${name}=${shellEscape(value)}\\\\n`, \\\"utf8\\\");\\r\\n}\\r\\n\\r\\nfunction cleanupSessionJobs(cwd, sessionId) {\\r\\n  if (!cwd || !sessionId) {\\r\\n    return;\\r\\n  }\\r\\n\\r\\n  const workspaceRoot = resolveWorkspaceRoot(cwd);\\r\\n  const stateFile = resolveStateFile(workspaceRoot);\\r\\n  if (!fs.existsSync(stateFile)) {\\r\\n    return;\\r\\n  }\\r\\n\\r\\n  const state = loadState(workspaceRoot);\\r\\n  const removedJobs = state.jobs.filter((job) => job.sessionId === sessionId);\\r\\n  if (removedJobs.length === 0) {\\r\\n    return;\\r\\n  }\\r\\n\\r\\n  for (const job of removedJobs) {\\r\\n    const stillRunning = job.status === \\\"queued\\\" || job.status === \\\"running\\\";\\r\\n    if (!stillRunning) {\\r\\n      continue;\\r\\n    }\\r\\n    try {\\r\\n      terminateProcessTree(job.pid ?? Number.NaN);\\r\\n    } catch {\\r\\n      // Ignore teardown failures during session shutdown.\\r\\n    }\\r\\n  }\\r\\n\\r\\n  saveState(workspaceRoot, {\\r\\n    ...state,\\r\\n    jobs: state.jobs.filter((job) => job.sessionId !== sessionId)\\r\\n  });\\r\\n}\\r\\n\\r\\nfunction handleSessionStart(input) {\\r\\n  appendEnvVar(SESSION_ID_ENV, input.session_id);\\r\\n  appendEnvVar(PLUGIN_DATA_ENV, process.env[PLUGIN_DATA_ENV]);\\r\\n}\\r\\n\\r\\nasync function handleSessionEnd(input) {\\r\\n  const cwd = input.cwd || process.cwd();\\r\\n  const brokerSession =\\r\\n    loadBrokerSession(cwd) ??\\r\\n    (process.env[BROKER_ENDPOINT_ENV]\\r\\n      ? {\\r\\n          endpoint: process.env[BROKER_ENDPOINT_ENV],\\r\\n          pidFile: process.env[PID_FILE_ENV] ?? null,\\r\\n          logFile: process.env[LOG_FILE_ENV] ?? null\\r\\n        }\\r\\n      : null);\\r\\n  const brokerEndpoint = brokerSession?.endpoint ?? null;\\r\\n  const pidFile = brokerSession?.pidFile ?? null;\\r\\n  const logFile = brokerSession?.logFile ?? null;\\r\\n  const sessionDir = brokerSession?.sessionDir ?? null;\\r\\n  const pid = brokerSession?.pid ?? null;\\r\\n\\r\\n  if (brokerEndpoint) {\\r\\n    await sendBrokerShutdown(brokerEndpoint);\\r\\n  }\\r\\n\\r\\n  cleanupSessionJobs(cwd, input.session_id || process.env[SESSION_ID_ENV]);\\r\\n  teardownBrokerSession({\\r\\n    endpoint: brokerEndpoint,\\r\\n    pidFile,\\r\\n    logFile,\\r\\n    sessionDir,\\r\\n    pid,\\r\\n    killProcess: terminateProcessTree\\r\\n  });\\r\\n  clearBrokerSession(cwd);\\r\\n}\\r\\n\\r\\nasync function main() {\\r\\n  const input = readHookInput();\\r\\n  const eventName = process.argv[2] ?? input.hook_event_name ?? \\\"\\\";\\r\\n\\r\\n  if (eventName === \\\"SessionStart\\\") {\\r\\n    handleSessionStart(input);\\r\\n    return;\\r\\n  }\\r\\n\\r\\n  if (eventName === \\\"SessionEnd\\\") {\\r\\n    await handleSessionEnd(input);\\r\\n  }\\r\\n}\\r\\n\\r\\nmain().catch((error) => {\\r\\n  process.stderr.write(`${error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error)}\\\\n`);\\r\\n  process.exit(1);\\r\\n});\",\"stderr\":\"\",\"interrupted\":false,\"isImage\":false,\"noOutputExpected\":false}"</outcome>

content.thinking:
This observation reveals the actual hook script content and configuration. The key findings:

1. The hook is configured in hooks.json to run on SessionStart and SessionEnd
2. The SessionEnd hook script performs several cleanup operations:
   - Sends broker shutdown signal
   - Cleans up session jobs
   - Terminates process trees
   - Clears broker session data

The script is async and has error handling. The "Hook cancelled" error could be due to:
- Timeout waiting for broker shutdown
- Issues with process termination
- File system operations failing
- The hook taking too long to complete

This is a discovery of how the hook works and what it's trying to do.

content.text:
```xml
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>SessionEnd Hook Implementation Details</title>
  <subtitle>Codex plugin SessionEnd hook performs broker shutdown, process cleanup, and session state teardown operations</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>SessionEnd hook executes async operations including sendBrokerShutdown, cleanupSessionJobs, and teardownBrokerSession</fact>
    <fact>Hook script reads stdin for hook input data and parses session_id and cwd from JSON</fact>
    <fact>cleanupSessionJobs filters jobs by session ID and terminates process trees for queued/running jobs</fact>
    <fact>Broker session data loaded from workspace state or environment variables BROKER_ENDPOINT_ENV, PID_FILE_ENV, LOG_FILE_ENV</fact>
    <fact>Hook uses terminateProcessTree to kill broker processes and cleans up pidFile, logFile, and sessionDir</fact>
    <fact>Script exits with code 1 on unhandled errors, writing error message to stderr</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The codex plugin's SessionEnd hook performs comprehensive cleanup operations when sessions terminate. It first attempts to send a graceful shutdown signal to a broker endpoint if one exists, then cleans up any jobs associated with the session ID by terminating their process trees, and finally tears down broker session resources (PID files, log files, session directories). The hook is asynchronous and involves network calls (sendBrokerShutdown), file system operations (reading state files, removing session data), and process management (terminateProcessTree). The "Hook cancelled" error likely occurs when these async operations exceed the hook timeout threshold or when one of the cleanup operations fails or hangs, causing the Claude Code runtime to cancel the hook execution prematurely.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>.claude/plugins/cache/openai-codex/codex/1.0.4/hooks/hooks.json</file>
    <file>.claude/plugins/cache/openai-codex/codex/1.0.4/scripts/session-lifecycle-hook.mjs</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```
